speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two, our survivor Shoshana Kahn, and today is June 10, 1996. How would your family have identified as Jews? Could you tell about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had Pesach, Hanukkah, and a Rosh Hashanah, and Yom Kippur. And as-- as I said, they were Jews. That's as much as I know. I knew I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what languages were spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only German.

speaker: interviewee
text: German. Definitely German. My mom was the one who talked Latvian without an accent, because she hails from a-- from a small town, Tukums. And she somehow, she has a Baltic mom. And there were very few Jews over there. And she was-- very few Jews talked Latvian without an accent. My mom was one of them who did. She had connections with Latvians. She understood those people much better than most Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have newspapers or radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, dear, we had a newspaper, which was called the in German. There was probably a Latvian newspaper, which I don't recall. I don't know which one-- what it was called. And we had a radio way, way back then. But in the very beginnings, I had a radio with a little stone and with a little needle. And it was squeaking and squawking. And then we had a real radio. Yes, indeed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a telephone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- we didn't have one. But my aunt had next door, because she had this business-- had a business. And a few years ago, I was in Israel, and I went to her place. And there was an old telephone book from Libau, and I found my aunt, Minna Firkser. It was very strange, very simple, very thin. Very few-- people who had businesses had telephones.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get news, basic news about what was happening in the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had the

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that current?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a daily newspaper or a weekly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a daily. And there was a-- was a radio. But that was the That's what we read. It must have been another one in Latvian too, but I don't remember. We had lots of-- of German magazines from Germany. I mean, that was the thing to do, read German. I mean, my first book I-- I read-- book-- my book-- first started reading from had Queen Victoria or what's-- Kaiser Wilhelm on the cover. And-- and she wore a bouquet of kornblumen. That was her flower-- Kaiser Wilhelm--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing before the war started? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to high school. One year was high school. It was the Soviet Union was there. We had-- the Soviet Union was in Latvia. They-- I think they took Latvia over in '39. And we-- I went-- went to high school then.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life change at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the Soviet Union? Not very much, really. It-- I found it very exciting. It was so different. Latvia, life was not amusing. It was not exciting. It was the same as the beach, the strawberries, and this on and on. And here, all of a sudden, there are trucks, and soldiers were dancing and music and [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] and what have you. And-- and-- and all of a sudden, our teachers were not any more teachers. They were comrades, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Comrade so and so and comrade so and so.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were the same teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same teachers turned into comrades, yes. And we had-- we had to-- we stopped talking German in school. They said-- they said, this is madness. You are Jews, live in Latvia, and talk German? Listen, you'd better talk Latvian or-- and study Yiddish and learn Yiddish, and-- which we then did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came home and said something in Yiddish to my mom. I remember, she blanched. That was not very elegant, she thought. Much did she know, poor thing. And what else is happening? Yes. My-- we were not, thank God, not very rich. So our house was not taken over by the state. But the businesses were taken over by the state, the big shops. How did it affect your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. It didn't affect at all. My mom kept on working, and my pop kept on working. And we lived in the same apartment, went to school, and had a new-- new subject, which was politics. And that was-- was not as simple as it sounded, because our teacher, whose name was Zaks, was a-- an ex-communist who spent a lot of time in prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, when he was released from prison before the Soviet Union, he had a wonderful little library and-- with fabulous books-- all the newest novels from Hungary in those days-- [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. And I used to go every other day through the library and change books. And one day, he looked at me with his steely eyes and says, when are you doing your homework if you read novels all the time? That man became my political instructor. And he was a tough guy. I do remember that one kid in our class's name was Gamza. He was a grandchild of my-- my grandma's husband, the one. [NON-ENGLISH] said-- he asked some questions and-- and [NON-ENGLISH] said, well, this is only propaganda. He wrote down the name of your parents, the name of your gran-- where do they work, where do they live? And it was one-- then we got an inkling one doesn't say everything one wants.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our then principal, Director Greenfeld, he was a very fine man. He was a Zionist. He talked Hebrew. His daughter [NON-ENGLISH] was in our class. She talked Russian. She talked Hebrew, was a dancer. She was lovely. He then was demoted from his principality. And somebody who worked with the-- with the Communist Party got the job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, we were on an outing with-- with the Director Greenfeld, ex-principal. And I remember we sat in-- in the woods. And he said, how will you relate now to Israel and to Zionism? And we were careful to say, yes, no. It-- it was painful for him, obviously.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Hebrew wasn't taught anymore. We had to do Yiddish. And we put up Yiddish plays. And it was amazingly interesting-- [NON-ENGLISH] Yiddish plays. And it was lovely. I never knew Yiddish was such an exciting language. It was really great-- great language. And I had a lot of fun studying that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your plans for the future as this young student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was the kind of a-- a girl who didn't have plans for the future. But all my other girlfriends did. [NON-ENGLISH] had plans for the future. Lenchen had plans for the future. I only wanted to have a good time and eventually end up in America with a white ticket fence-- picket fence, rather. I-- I was convinced that-- there was a movie with Mickey Rooney, with something Hardy.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Hardy Boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. There was Judy-- oh. There was Judy Garland. And there was somebody else, and-- and Mickey Rooney. There was a family. Mr. Stone was the father. Well, there was this movie I went to see always it. I thought, in America, everybody lives in a place with a white picket fence and rides a bike.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then there was this marvelous, marvelous American actress called Deanna Durbin-- before your time. You ever heard-- hear of her? She was a coloratura. She was great. And then if you get a movie-- that movie we were allowed to see it, because it dealt with unions, with musicians in America were not unionized and didn't get good-- good salaries.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and all that happened in-- in Carnegie Hall. There was Leopold Stokowski conducting the orchestra-- little Deanna Durbin, who wore like a [NON-ENGLISH], a little hat and a bolero and white saddle shoes. She was-- she was a daughter of one of the musicians who didn't get to a salary high enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and of course, I wanted to be Deanna Durbin. Of course, my aunt made me the dress with the bolero, with the cap and all that. And there is a scene in that movie. Mischa Auer was wonderful actor with big, round-- hey, you guys. You don't know who Mischa Auer is? Leopold Stokowski you know. OK. So Leopold Stokowski was conducting the-- his-- his orchestra. And out comes Deanna Durbin and is on one of the first tier to the left. She stands there and she breaks into song-- 'Ave Maria' by Gounod. And Leopold Stokowski is dumbfounded. That voice-- he never heard a voice like that. And then, of course, he starts to conducting and the-- it's so beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the musicians got raises. It was lovely. And then-- every time I go to Carnegie Hall, which is quite often, I greet Deanna Durbin up there. And-- I say, [INAUDIBLE]. And then I try telling people, you know that Deanna Durbin-- they don't know what I'm talking about. The lady lives now in Paris and is married to a doctor and is quite fat. I was so disappointed. She had a marvelous voice, Deanna Durbin. You must get one of those movies. She's a lovely lady. It was a kid-- she was a teenager when she did it all. [SINGING] And a bicycle built for two-- they did that. Mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the rest of the words?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not quite. But I remember them-- and a bicycle built for two. And doing-- doing 'Ave Maria' by Gounod. That was so great. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Well, it's about-- what did I do? I mean, as a kid-- in summer, we went to the symphony. And my-- my parents took me along. And once, I understand that Vladislav Huberman-- I never heard of him-- was the biggest violinist of those days. Vladislav Huberman-- and the whole town was just jittery. And Huberman, Huberman, Huberman, and we are going to hear Huberman. And I was a little kid, and I thought it was a Buberman. But I didn't want to go to the symphony that night. And my parents took me there. And I sat there in between them, and the woman was playing beautifully. It was a beautiful place. This was outdoors in a big shell underneath the chestnut trees. And it is-- it's funny, you know, how you-- the-- how I associate things. When I hear the cello solo from the-- from Mendelssohn's [NON-ENGLISH], well, I hear the chestnuts rustling in the-- in the-- the breeze. And after the in between, in the intermission, we walked up and down. We met people. And after the concert, everybody went to the pavilion for cake and coffee and ice cream-- not bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They're very nicely turned out everybody, very nicely dressed, since it was a city of tailors. And we were very fashion oriented.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the fabric come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wouldn't know-- from all sorts of places. They had lovely silks, lovely stuff, really lovely clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing which-- which-- nothing, really, which I remember. There were-- Latvians didn't love us much, I understand. And I understand why. I mean, it was their country, and we were-- we were very friendly with the Germans. We had the-- the-- the tennis courts and the sail club-- sailing club was-- there, the Jews and the Germans were members, and the Latvians were not. I tell you, they were just peasants and maids. I don't remember anti-Semitism towards my persona. So I cannot tell you much about anti-Semitism before. We were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the Latvians in business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the Latvians in business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, but we have-- I don't know any of them. Probably there were some. Maybe there were some doctors even. I don't know anybody. We had-- yes, we had teachers, the Latvians. We had Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH], who was-- she did Latvian history. And we had a Latvian art teacher, who taught us every year the same thing, which are the primary colors-- red, [GERMAN]. Everything the same, everything-- always the same. We didn't study much-- a boring guy. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the rise of the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we went-- that-- that's an interesting question. Good question. We went-- we often did-- we went on picnics, you see. And somebody from Germany-- all of a sudden-- that went like that-- people, young people, came from Germany, Jews, and came to-- to Libau. There were lots of young German Jewish men who came on Hachshara, if you know what that is. Before they go to Israel, they had to learn a profession, which is-- which is used-- you can use in Palestine. That's called preparing. Hachshara is to be prepared. So many young German Jews came from Germany and came to Latvia, where they learned to do this or that or something else. From then-- from there, they went to Palestine. And it was-- of course, they were Germans. They were the-- just the most wonderful boys in the whole wide world. Their names was Walter and their names was Manfred and [GERMAN]. Anyhow, some of them married some of the ladies. And my-- my aunt had the Manfred Kahn, a gentleman friend, who was glorious and went to Palestine and so on and on. And other young German kids came in the '30s, and they were in our class. And one brought-- and then they brought a book, which was called the [GERMAN], the brown book. And that was-- they had photographs of the-- of what happens in-- in Germany, which was a book which was absolutely verboten in Germany. They brought it in. And you could see the picture, how they tortured Jews and people in Sachsenhausen and in prison and what, on and on and on. And that put the goose bumps on us, when we looked at those pictures, the brown book. And we read the newspaper. And everybody was-- I heard the parents talking about Mr. Roosevelt was going to-- to-- to help us all and whatnot. I know my-- my piano teacher's son came from-- from Germany and stayed and either thought they were going to escape or postpone this whole thing. But it-- it wasn't much good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have people from other areas coming in with reports about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Germany is where it happened. Germany, in those days, it were-- it was where it happened, in Germany, my Germany, our Germany, with the queen and the king. Whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people believe all the reports that they were hearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- yes. Well, we had the photographs to-- to prove that. And my aunt came back from Danzig with horror stories, [NON-ENGLISH] Paula. She couldn't function there anymore. Danzig was then-- the [GERMAN] Danzig was very Nazi-oriented. It's Gdansk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the stories she told?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But they were probably the same stories which everybody knew. I don't remember her telling, but I know she-- she left Danzig and came back to Libau for obvious reasons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly. No. I only know she left-- she came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you first aware of the German presence and occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand the question.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did--

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Germans moved-- when the war broke out and the German occupied the Libau? In June sometimes, in '41, or what? Yes? June?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, the war-- they attacked the Russians in front of Liepaja. You know, there's Ribbentrop, Stalin Pact, and then they attacked the Russia-- the Russians. And it was in-- in Libau. The ships attacked the ship. And there was some bombing. And were in basements. And the bombing was quite fierce, and some houses were destroyed and so on. [NON-ENGLISH] Paula was with us. And we sat there in the basement in the big house. And I read by a candle. Tara was burning. I was so upset. And then the-- and then the war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it last?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not terribly long. I cannot even tell you, not terribly long. That started differently. And the day when-- in June when we were meant to have our tests in class-- chemistry or which was, it was in June. And Ossie, my friend who was such a genius, used to help me with the tests. And he worked with me all night with the test for the next day. And then he said-- it was a very unpleasant atmosphere, very tense. You couldn't put your finger on it, but it was tense. And then Ossie left about 1:00-ish in the morning. He thought I was prepared enough. And then under the window, he sang a song in Russian, which it goes-- [SINGING IN RUSSIAN]. Say goodbye from each other-- at the door. It could-- it could be forever. [RUSSIAN]-- maybe forever. He sang-- he had a wonderful baritone. And that's when he left. And the next day, I wanted to go to school. And things were so strange. There were lots of soldiers and police on the street. Anyhow, they had-- the Soviet-- the-- the Russians deported all the people who were-- who they considered a danger to the politics or to the war. That means the bourgeois, the Jews who were rich, the Jews who were Zionists, I mean, who-- whom they could. They were deported to Russia in big-- in trains, in sealed trains. And Ossie was one among them. He-- during the-- during the time of the Soviet Union, we couldn't have our big apartments. We have to share so-- so and so much square feet was allowed, and so on. And they had a very big apartment, the Velkins. And they had somebody from the NKVD living there with them. And they got the info, so what was going on. Besides, I didn't tell you that was a-- the that house, which was called the Blue Wonder, the Blue Miracle-- underneath, in the cellar, they had the NKVD doing evil things to people-- that's Blaue Wunder

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew that, what was going on. That we knew-- in Blaue Wunder-- some wonder, it had three stories. And-- and that NKVD of man-- in those days, it was called NKVD-- came then the next morning and brought me things Ossie left me. He left me 50 rubles, all his savings, his fountain pen, and a lovely book by-- not [NON-ENGLISH], by-- it's called with illustrations by some Dutchman. Very-- do you ever seen that? It's a wonderful-- it's 'wonder' something. Anyhow-- so-- and that was a goodbye. And I was so upset. I cried so hard. I took my bike and drove out to the trains, but they were all sealed. And never saw those people for 50 years. Two years ago, he came to visit with his wife. Yeah. He brought me the picture. Never heard of them-- they were all in Siberia. But then afterwards, they-- his mom died. His-- but they went to school, and he became an architect. And he married an architect. And he has a daughter who's an architect and a son-in-law who's an architect and has two grandchildren, lives in Minsk, and it's very tough now. If I can, I send always money with somebody who goes back and forth. He was-- he really was a genius. I have the picture here. And he used to draw fabulous. Maths was his forte, and-- and languages. He-- until today, he can recite Schiller and Goethe without-- and very clever man, very smart man. And he was deported. And then I went to school, and half of the class wasn't there. And we had no tests. This time, I wasn't happy. Half of the class were-- it was empty. No test, no people, nothing-- that's when it really started to get tough. And then the bombings started. And when it was quiet, the Germans were in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who they were, what soldiers, what type of uniforms they wore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans? Wehrmacht-- grey uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: What orders did they give? Did they have to speak to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you had the-- the usual things with everybody went through. Jews were not allowed to have radios. Jews were not-- our bicycles they had to take away. And we had-- no more jobs for us. And we couldn't work. We couldn't-- then after-- it then escalated. Then Jews couldn't go-- had the curfew. They could be in the street only certain hours. And then they escalated even more. We had to wear the green-- the yellow-- yellow star, which is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what the star looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yellow-- yellow-- yellow Magen David with Jew written in it. I had a very elegant one. My [NON-ENGLISH] made me one of silk. It had to be in the front and had to be in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it attached?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sewn on. And we are not-- we're not meant to walk on the sidewalk, only in the middle of the street with the horses, which was good company. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel the first time you went out with your star?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot tell you how I felt. The whole thing was so surrealistic, it was like a play. We had to put the star. I had a star, I had the pretty star, not the pretty star. I mean, it was so crazy, so absurd. I mean, I did-- I was not ashamed to be Jewish, for sure. I was only amazed that I had a girlfriend from my class, and she was half Jewish. Her mother was German and her father was Jewish. Her name was Inge Loevius. And she walked on the sidewalk, when I walked in the middle of the street. And you know, if-- I don't want to sound arrogant. If-- I mean, she looked like her father, not like her mother. And I said, hi, Inge. And she wouldn't talk to me. That was not nice. Well, I-- I was-- I was taken aback. We were such good friends before. I mean, really-- used to exchange books and things. She had lots of books, and I had lots of books. And now, all of a sudden, she doesn't know me. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did other people treat you, the other non-Jews that you may have had contact with before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have contact with the non-Jews. I didn't. My mom maybe had. I did not. I didn't have contact with non-Jews. That was it-- didn't know anybody. And nobody know-- knew me. I mean, it-- it was when-- when-- we had to come every morning on the [NON-ENGLISH], where they collected the Jews and ordered us to go to work. Lots of-- of Jewish people, didn't know that they was Jewish. I only knew if they had a bike--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so then, we were surprised. That one is Jewish? I didn't know that that one is Jewish. Oh, that one is Jewish too? Oh, yeah. And all sorts of things happened one after another. Then my-- it was July-- and my father-- we had to go-- we had to go-- go work. We had to go to the [NON-ENGLISH]-- What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the-- there were-- we were-- had to stand there, and then the Germans came and picked us out for jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: '41. And we went to work with-- for the army. I remember working some gardens. Some girls were cleaning the-- the barracks, washing, cleaning, and did things like that. And of course, the Jews had to bury the-- the corpses and the stones, you know, when the-- from the bombing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Very, very often, men went to work and never came back. 4,000 Jews were, in July, killed on the beach in the lighthouse. My father was among them. They had to dig their own graves and-- and were shot down in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- but we didn't know it then. We thought they were-- then there came the rumors, you know? Oh, we saw them here. We saw them there. We saw them here. We saw them there. Yeah? And then came rumors they were shot. And then [INAUDIBLE] no, they were-- they were sighted here and there. And then the hopes went up and down. And when my pop didn't come back, my-- my-- my mother said-- said to me, oh, your father has-- has only those heavy shoes, and he's going to work. Why don't you go to the prison and bring him some light shoes? Well, my mama said-- Mom said. I go off to the prison, ring the bell of the prison, and a little old Latvian guard opens the door. And I said, I'm looking for my father. I want to give him some sandals. And he said, will you run home? Will you run home right now? And I came running home. I mean, we had no idea what was going on. My mom said, why don't you go-- I mean, just the idea when somebody's young and so nothing is going to happen to that person-- why don't you go to the estate to the-- and ask for where my father is? I went there. I mean, honestly. I-- I-- I went there and-- and asked stupid questions and was thrown out. I was not-- [INAUDIBLE] what was going on. When did you learn the truth?

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned the truth, but we didn't want to believe it. We learned the truth. We knew the truth. But we choose not to believe it. Who wants-- can believe that?

speaker: interviewer
text: When the people were picked up on the streets and disappeared, we called it [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was a certain [BACKOUND NOISE]  little car, the little truck, we saw it coming. Yeah? That was when they [NON-ENGLISH] the Jews. And they took them out to the-- to the-- [NON-ENGLISH] or where-- where they were then executed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Latvians were enormously active in that. There was a pilot called [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a wonder-- the hero of Latvia. He was a head of the [NON-ENGLISH]. This was the Latvian ice hockey. They were very happy to get rid of the Russians and [INAUDIBLE] they received the Germans with flowers. And they waved from the thing. And of course, they were extremely happy to deliver us. They didn't-- they showed what they thought of us all the time at that point. We had no chance to hide anywhere. There were very few Latvians, some, who would have helped us to hide or not-- not reported us. It wasn't-- it was not happening in Libau too much-- very little. Here and there, one, not what happened in other countries-- it was-- they were extremely happy to cooperate with the-- it was so bad that they wrote-- the German army, the head of the German army wrote a letter to Berlin and asked them to stop the senseless killings, the tortures of the Jews, which is not acceptable for them, and that the-- the German army gets demoralized by whatever's happening to the mass-- mass killings and the-- this whole bloodshed. That was in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food situation at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- we could only shop in the Jewish store, of course. And we got Jewish-- for Jewish allotments. And in the they usually were writing what we-- what we will get next week or what have you, yeah? But we could-- more or less, we were-- everybody-- I mean, if we wouldn't have been shot, we would have managed. You would sell one's-- one's plates, or sell one's jewelry, and get for that. And then, there was a German lady, Fraulein-- there was a German lady who worked for our-- for the-- our neighbors [NON-ENGLISH] person. She was-- she loved him very much, Mr. Edelstein. And she was his secretary for many, many years. And she came after curfew. And she went to all the Jews she knew, especially to us, whom she knew personally, and she brought us delicacies, which she cooked, yeah, and cookies, and marinated mushrooms. I remember that very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I do, but not right now. [NON-ENGLISH] And she-- and she took all our jewelry--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop the tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fraulein-- oh, but--
